risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to either the hydro-cortisone (n = 31: 21 males, 10 females) or placebo (n = 33: 21 males, 12 females) groups." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g., computer random number generator, random number table, coin toss, permuted blocks with a stated random method). Under Cochrane criteria, this is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, so the risk is unclear.

Summary: The paper states that participants were "randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course ... of hydrocortisone or placebo," but it does not describe any method used to conceal allocation from the recruiter/enrolling staff. No explicit central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is reported. Comment: Randomized and double-blind describes sequence/treatment blinding, not whether the next assignment could be foreseen before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo" and "Following consent, the nurse administered the first oral dose [20 mg hydrocortisone ... or placebo capsules]" Comment: The study explicitly states it was double-blind and used placebo capsules, indicating participants and the personnel administering treatment were intended to be blinded. The outcomes assessed were PTSD symptoms, depression, and quality of life at follow-up; although these could be influenced by knowledge of allocation, the reported blinding makes it unlikely that allocation awareness systematically affected the outcomes.

Quote: "Participants were administered the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS) ... Self-report measures included the Center for Epidemiological Studies—Depression Scale and the SF-36" and "randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either ... hydrocortisone or placebo." Comment: The main PTSD outcome was assessed by a clinician-administered structured interview (CAPS), implying an independent assessor rather than patient self-report. The trial was described as double-blind, and for these interview-based outcomes it is unlikely that knowledge of allocation would have influenced outcome measurement. The self-report outcomes (CES-D, SF-36) are participant-reported and thus subject to participant blinding, but the key clinician-assessed PTSD outcome is adequately protected against detection bias in this trial.

Quote: "There was no differential drop out between the hydrocortisone and placebo groups. At 1 month posttrauma, 6 participants had dropped from the placebo group and 7 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group, while at 3 months a total of 9 had dropped from the placebo group and 12 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group. Of the 13 participants who dropped between randomization and the 1-month follow-up, one complained of dizziness and asked to discontinue participation (from the hydrocortisone group), and the remaining 12 could not be contacted after numerous attempts... Drop-outs between the 1- and 3- month time points were all due to inability to reach participants after repeated attempts. There were no differences between drop-outs and participants who were retained through the protocol on any study variable." Comment: Attrition was moderate but balanced across arms, reasons were mostly loss to follow-up rather than outcome-related, and the authors report no differences between completers and dropouts. This makes attrition bias unlikely for this domain.
